# Code of Conduct

This Code of Conduct is adapted from the Contributor Covenant v2.0 and modified to reflect the scale, reality, and operating model of this project.

## Purpose

The purpose of this Code of Conduct is to establish a minimum set of rules that allow technical discussions and contributions to thrive without personal attacks, unproductive arguments, or maintainer burnout.

This document:
- Is not a declaration intended to eliminate all conflict.
- Does not aim to protect all opinions equally.

Rather, its primary purpose is to clearly block behaviors where attacks on people undermine technical discussion.

## Our Pledge

All participants in this project (users, contributors, reviewers, and maintainers) commit to the following:
- Maintaining an environment where individuals are respected regardless of background, level of experience, or identity.
- Allowing robust criticism of code and design, but never attacks against people.
- Framing discussions around solving technical problems, not “winning” arguments.

## Expected Behavior

The following behaviors are expected within this project:
- Criticism and discussion based strictly on technical reasoning.
- Respect for participants holding different technical opinions.
- Acknowledging mistakes and gracefully correcting them.
- Creating issues and pull requests with sufficient context and reproducible steps.
- Acting with consideration for the long-term sustainability of the project and the time of its maintainers.

These points may sound obvious, but they are written here because they are vital for a healthy open-source ecosystem.

## Unacceptable Behavior

The following behaviors are explicitly not tolerated:
- Insults, mockery, personal attacks, or demeaning language.
- Sexualized language, hate speech, or threats.
- Repeated trolling or deliberate disruption of discussion.
- Forcing political or ideological debates into technical issues.
- Publishing personal or private information without consent.
- Making excessive demands of maintainers or contributors, or acting with an attitude of entitlement.

**In particular:** The assumption that *“because this is open source, immediate support or custom features should be provided for free”* is not respected in this project.

## Communication Guidelines

- Issues and pull requests should be created within the appropriate scope of the project.
- Questions and bug reports must include the minimum information necessary to reproduce the issue.
- Do not unnecessarily expand the scope of discussion within a single issue (avoid "issue hijacking").
- Repeated questions, low-effort requests, or pressuring messages may be closed or ignored.

Please remember: Not receiving an immediate response does not grant the right to behave rudely. Maintainers have lives outside of this project.

## Enforcement

Project maintainers reserve the right to:
- Edit or remove inappropriate issues, pull requests, or comments.
- Issue warnings or restrict participation.
- Permanently ban participants in cases of repeated or malicious behavior.

Maintainers are not neutral arbitrators; they are responsible for the health of the project. While you may disagree with a decision, it is not subject to public debate once a final call is made.

## Reporting

Clear violations of this Code of Conduct should not be debated publicly in GitHub Issues to avoid unnecessary drama. Instead, please report them directly to the project maintainer via Discord (my username is available on my GitHub profile). 
    
Not all disputes warrant maintainer intervention, but legitimate abuse will be handled discretely and decisively.

## Scope

This Code of Conduct applies to all interactions within GitHub Issues, Pull Requests, Discussions, and any community spaces related to TinyMQ.
